## Supplementary Materials to ' Fairness-aware Online Price Discrimination with Nonparametric Demand Models'

## EC.1. Proof Omitted in Section 4

## EC.1.1. Proof of Theorem 1 for ExploreUnconstrainedOPT

In this subsection, we establish the theoretical guarantee for ExploreUnconstrainedOPT in Theorem 1.

First, the following lemma upper bounds the number of time periods used by the algorithm.

Lemma EC.1. Each invocation of Algorithm 2 spends at most O ( K 4 p 2 C 2 T 4 5 log T log( pT )) selling periods, where only a universal constant is hidden in the O ( · ) notation.

Proof of Lemma EC.1. It is easy to verify that the length of the trisection interval p R -p L shrinks by a factor of 2 / 3 after each iteration, and therefore there are at most log 3 / 2 (( p -p ) T 1 / 5 ) = O (log( pT )) iterations. Also note that within each iteration, the algorithm uses at most O ( K 4 p 2 C 2 T 4 / 5 log T ) selling periods. The lemma then follows. □

We then turn to upper bound the estimation error of the algorithm. For each iteration r , we define the following event

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Let r ∗ be the last iteration. We note that 1) A 0 always holds, 2) A r ∗ , if holds, would imply the desired estimation error bound ( | ̂ p ♯ z -p ♯ z | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 ). Therefore, to prove the desired error bound in Theorem 1, we first prove the following lemma.

Lemma EC.2. For each r ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , r ∗ } , we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof of Lemma EC.2. Given the event A r -1 , we focus on iteration r . During this iteration, by Azuma's inequality, we first have that for each trisection point i ∈{ 1 , 2 } , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The rest of the proof will be conditioned on that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which happens with probability at least (1 -4 T -2 ) by a union bound.

To establish A r , let p L and p R be the values taken at the beginning of iteration r , and we discuss the following three cases.

Case 1: p ♯ z ∈ [ p m 1 , p m 2 ] . A r automatically holds in this case.

Case 2: p ♯ z ∈ [ p L , p m 1 ) . In this case, by Line 7 of the algorithm, to establish A r , we need to show that ̂ d m 1 ( p m 1 -c ) &gt; ̂ d m 2 ( p m 2 -c ). By Item (a) of Assumption 1, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Also, by Item (b) of Assumption 1, when d z ( p ♯ ) &gt;d z ( p m 1 ) &gt;d z ( p m 2 ), we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where in the last inequality we applied Eq. (EC.2). Together with Eq. (EC.1), we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, A r holds in this case.

Case 3: p ♯ z ∈ ( p m 2 , p R ] . This case can be similarly handled as Case 2 by symmetry.

Combining the 3 cases above, the lemma is proved. □

Finally, since r ∗ ≤ O (log( pT )), we have that A r ∗ holds with probability at least 1 -O ( T -2 log( pT )). Together with Lemma EC.1, we prove Theorem 1.

## EC.1.2. Proof of Theorem 2 for ExploreConstrainedOPT

First, the following lemma upper bounds the number of time periods used by the algorithm.

Lemma EC.3. Algorithm 3 uses at most O ( pT 3 / 5 ln T ) selling periods, where only a universal constant is hidden in the O ( · ) notation.

Proof. For each price checkpoint ℓ j , the algorithm uses at most 6 T 2 / 5 ln T selling periods. Since there are J = ⌈ ( p -p ) T 1 / 5 ⌉ selling price checkpoints, the total selling periods used by the algorithm are at most O ( pT 3 / 5 ln T ). □

We next turn to prove the (near-)optimality of the estimated prices ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 . To this end, we first establish the following key relation between the price gap of the constrained optimal solution and that of the unconstrained optimal solution.

Lemma EC.4. p ∗ 1 -p ∗ 2 = λ ( p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 2 ) .

Proof. In this proof we assume without loss of generality that p ♯ 1 ≤ p ♯ 2 as the other case can be similarly handled by symmetry.

Since R 1 ( d ) is a unimodal function and d 1 ( p ) is a monotonically decreasing function, we have that R 1 ( p ) = R 1 ( d 1 ( p )) is a unimodal function. Similarly, R 2 ( p ) = R 2 ( d 2 ( p )) is also a unimodal function. Under the price fairness constraint, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We first claim that p ∗ 1 ≤ p ♯ 2 , since otherwise (when p ∗ 1 &gt;p ♯ 2 ) the objective value of the feasible solution ( p 1 , p 2 ) = ( p ♯ 2 , p ♯ 2 ) is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first inequality is due to the unimodality of R 1 ( p ). This contradicts to the optimality of ( p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 ).

We also claim that p ∗ 1 ≥ p ♯ 1 , since otherwise (when p ∗ 1 &lt;p ♯ 1 ) we have p ∗ 2 + p ♯ 1 -p ∗ 1 ≤ p ♯ 1 + λ ( p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 1 ) ≤ p ♯ 2 and p ∗ 2 + p ♯ 1 -p ∗ 1 &gt;p ∗ 2 , and the objective value of the feasible solution ( p 1 , p 2 ) = ( p ♯ 1 , p ∗ 2 + p ♯ 1 -p ∗ 1 ) is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the second inequality is due to the unimodality of R 2 ( p ). This also contradicts to the optimality of ( p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 ).

To summarize, we have shown that p ∗ 1 ∈ [ p ♯ 1 , p ♯ 2 ].

Since R 1 ( p 1 ) is monotonically decreasing when p 1 ∈ [ p ♯ 1 , p ♯ 2 ], by Eq. (EC.4), we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here, we use [ a ± b ] to denote the interval [ a -b, a + b ] for any a ∈ R and b ≥ 0.

In a similar way, we can also work with p ∗ 2 and show that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Combining Eq. (EC.5) and Eq. (EC.6), we conclude that p ∗ 1 -p ∗ 2 = λ ( p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 2 ) and the lemma is proved. □

The following lemma provide bounds for the ξ parameter which is used in the algorithm to control the price gaps between the two customer groups.

Lemma EC.5. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 1 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 and | ̂ p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 2 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 , we have that λξ ≤ λ | p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 2 | and λξ ≥ max { 0 , λ | p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 2 | -16 T -1 / 5 } .

Proof. We first have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We also have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The following lemma shows that our discretization scheme always guarantees that there is a price check point to approximate the constrained optimal prices.

Lemma EC.6. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 1 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 and | ̂ p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 2 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 , there exists ˜ j ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } such that both p 1 ( ˜ j ) , p 2 ( ˜ j ) ∈ [ p, p ] and | p 1 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 1 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 , | p 2 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 2 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 .

Proof. Consider ˜ j =argmin j | ℓ j -( p ∗ 1 + p ∗ 2 ) / 2 | , we have that | ℓ ˜ j -( p ∗ 1 + p ∗ 2 ) / 2 | ≤ T -1 / 5 . Now, by Lemma EC.4 and Lemma EC.5, we have that | ℓ ˜ j -λξ 2 -p ∗ 1 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 and | ℓ ˜ j + λξ 2 -p ∗ 2 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 . Therefore, we also have that | p 1 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 1 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 and | p 2 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 2 | ≤ 9 T -1 / 5 . □

We now prove the following lemma for the (near-)optimality of the estimated constrained prices.

Lemma EC.7. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 1 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 and | ̂ p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 2 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 , with probability (1 -4( p -p ) T -2 ) we have that R 1 ( ̂ p ∗ 1 ) + R 2 ( ̂ p ∗ 2 ) ≥ R 1 ( p ∗ 1 ) + R 2 ( p ∗ 2 ) -(4 + 18 K ) pT -1 / 5 .

Proof. By Azuma's inequality, for each j ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } , with probability 1 -4 T -3 , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, by a union bound, Eq. (EC.7) holds for each j ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } with probability at least 1 -4 JT -3 ≥ 1 -4( p -p ) T -2 . Conditioned on this event, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

With Eq. (EC.8), and let ˜ j be the index designated by Lemma EC.6, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

By Lemma EC.6 and Item (a) of Assumption 1, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Combining Eq. (EC.9) and Eq. (EC.10), we prove the lemma. □

We are now ready to prove Theorem 2. Note that the sample complexity is upper bounded due to Lemma EC.3. So long as | ̂ p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 1 | ≤ T -1 / 5 and | ̂ p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 2 | ≤ T -1 / 5 , the price fairness is always satisfied due to the first inequality shown in Lemma EC.5. Finally, the (near-)optimality of the estimated prices ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 is guaranteed by Lemma EC.7.

## EC.2. Proof of Theorem 3

Proof of Theorem 3. The proof will be carried out conditioned on the desired events of both Theorem 1 and Theorem 2, which happens with probability at least (1 -O ( T -1 )). We can first easily verify that the first 2 steps of Algorithm 1 satisfy the fairness constraint; and the 3rd step also satisfies the fairness constraint since | ̂ p ∗ 1 -̂ p ∗ 2 | ≤ λ | p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 2 | by Theorem 2. We then turn to bound the regret of the algorithm. Since the first two steps use at most O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) time periods, they incur at most O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) regret. By the desired event of Theorem 2, the regret incurred by the third step is at most T × O ( T -1 5 ) = O ( T 4 5 ). □

## EC.3. Proof Omitted in Section 5

## EC.4. Three-stage Framework may not Achieve Better-thanT 4 / 5 Regret for Linear Demands

This section explains that even with the linear demand functions, we may not hope to improve the T 4 / 5 -type regret under the three-stage framework, as stated at the end of Section 6.

We first explain that for the linear demand functions considered in Cohen et al. (2021), the optimal learning (convergence) rate of the unconstrained optimal prices is still ˜ Θ( a -1 / 4 ( T )) rather than ˜ Θ( a -1 / 2 ( T )) (i.e., the linear demand class does not help improve the learning of the constraint optimal prices).

By Theorem 1 of Keskin &amp; Zeevi (2014), we know the lower bound of dynamic pricing with unknown linear demand over ˜ Θ( a ( T )) periods is Ω( a 1 / 2 ( T )), which implies that for any anticipate policy π ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Invoking the smoothness property of R ( p ), we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where C is a positive absolute constant. Combining the above two inequalities, we could get the conclusion that for any anticipate policy π ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, the regret of the aforementioned three-stage framework will ultimately incur regret ˜ O ( T 4 / 5 ), just like the nonparametric demand.

## EC.5.1. Proof of Theorem 5 for ExploreConstrainedOPTGeneral

First, the following lemma upper bounds the number of the selling periods used by the algorithm:

Lemma EC.9. Algorithm 5 uses at most O ( pT 3 5 log T ) selling periods, where only a universal constant is hidden in O ( · ) notation.

Proof. For each price checkpoint ℓ j the algorithm uses at most 6 T 2 5 ln T selling periods. Since there are J = ⌈ ( p -p ) T 1 5 ⌉ selling price checkpoints, the total number of selling periods used by the algorithm is at most O ( pT 3 5 log T ). □

We then turn to upper bound the penalized regret incurred by the estimated prices ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 . Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

To upper bound this quantity, and noting that both ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 are selected from the discretized price checkpoints { ℓ j } j ∈{ 1 , 2 ,...,J } , we first prove the following lemma which shows that it suffices to choose the prices from the discretized price checkpoints. In other words, Lemma EC.10 upper bounds the regret due to the discretization method.

Observing that the Left-Hand-Side of Eq. (11) equals to G ( p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 ) -G ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 ), we prove the theorem. □

## EC.5.2. Proof of Theorem 6

Proof of Theorem 6. The proof will be carried out conditioned on the desired events of both Theorem 1 and Theorem 5, which happens with probability at least (1 -O ( T -1 )). Since the first two steps use at most O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) selling periods, they incur at most O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) × O ( p + M ) = O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) penalized regret. By the desired event of Theorem 5, the penalized regret incurred by the third step is at most T × O ( T -1 5 ) = O ( T 4 5 ). □

## EC.6. Lower Bound for Penalized Regret under the Soft Constraints

In Section 5, we presented the Ω( T 4 / 5 ) lower bound of the standard regret, assuming the hard fairness constraint is satisfied. In this section, we aim to establish a lower bound for the expected penalized regret under the soft constraint. Formally, we prove the following lower bound theorem.

Theorem EC.1. Suppose that π is an online pricing algorithm. Then for any λ ∈ ( ϵ, 1 -ϵ ) , γ ≥ 2 /A and T ≥ ϵ -C LB (where C LB &gt; 0 is the same universal constant as in Theorem 4), there exists a pricing instance such that the expected penalized regret of π is at least 1 200 ϵ 2 T 4 / 5 .

In the proof of Theorem EC.1, we use the same hard instances in Section 5, and thus Lemma 1 and Lemma 2 still hold. Specifically, we construct two problem instances I = I ( d 1 , d 3 ) and I ′ = I ( d 2 , d 3 ), where d i ( p ) = R i ( p ) /p for i ∈{ 1 , 2 , 3 } , and we define R i 's as follows.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here, A ≥ 1 is a large enough universal constant and h ≥ 0 depends on T , both of which will be chosen later. For any problem instance J ∈ {I , I ′ } , and any demand function d that is employed by a customer group in J , we denote by p ∗ ( d ; J ) the price for the customer group in the optimal fairness-aware clairvoyant solution to J .

The proof of Theorem EC.1 follows the similar proof structure of Theorem 4. In order to deal with the fact that the pricing policy does not satisfy the fairness constraints, the major differences between the proof of Theorem EC.1 compared with that of Theorem 4 include: 1) in the proof of Lemma EC.12 and Lemma EC.13, by imposing the carefully designed penalty term we establish the price of choosing a cheap first-group price and violating the fairness constraints; 2) in the proof of the main theorem, we discuss two more sub-cases when the probability distributions are close enough in two problem instances.

The price of a cheap first-group price. By Lemma 2, we see that when h ≤ ϵ 2 / 40, we have that both p ∗ ( d 1 ; I ) and p ∗ ( d 2 ; I ′ ) are greater than 1 + 7 √ h 4 . For any pricing strategy ( p, p ′ ), we say it is cheap for the first group if p ≤ 1 + 7 √ h 4 . The following lemma lower bounds the regret of a fairness-aware pricing strategy when it is cheap for the first group (and therefore deviates from the optimal solution).

Lemma EC.12. Suppose that h ≤ ϵ 4 / 400 and γ ≥ 2 /A . For any pricing strategy ( p 1 , p 3 ) for the problem instance I = I ( d 1 , d 3 ) , if p 1 ∈ [1 , 1 + 7 √ h 4 ] , we have that

## EC.7. Extension: the General Discrepancy Function

In the main body of this paper, we have aimed at achieving fairness via mandating the small difference between the prices (or other fairness measures defined by M ( · )). In this extension, we consider a general discrepancy function f ( · , · ) between the prices (or other fairness measures) to define the fairness constraints. Specifically, we use | f ( M 1 ( p 1 ) , M 2 ( p 2 )) | to substitute | M 1 ( p 1 ) -M 2 ( p 2 ) | in the fairness constraint and the penalized regret definition, and show that our algorithmic framework can be adapt to work with this class of even more general fairness constraints.

Let { p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 } denote the fairness-aware clairvoyant solution, i.e., the optimal solution to the following static optimization problem,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The regret is defined as the difference between the expected total revenue and the fairness-aware clairvoyant solution:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where p ∗ 1 and p ∗ 2 are the fairness-aware clairvoyant solutions defined in Eq. (EC.37).

For general fairness measure, the seller aims to minimize the following cumulative penalized regret:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We impose the following assumptions on the function f ( x,y ),

Assumption EC.1. The discrepancy function f ( x,y ) : [0 , M ] × [0 , M ] ↦→ R satisfies

- (a) When x = y , it holds that f ( x,y ) = 0 .
- (b) Lipschitz continuous property :

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- (c) Monotonicity property : | f ( x,y ) | increases as | x -y | increases, and for any fixed x .
- (d) Fix p 1 , for some constant ξ &gt; 0 , we could get p 2 &gt;p 1 such that | f ( p 1 , p 2 ) | = ξ , and we denote p 2 as f -1 ( p 1 ; ξ ) .

It is easy to note that f ( x,y ) = x -y considered in this paper satisfies the above assumptions. Meanwhile, some non-additive form of f ( x,y ) such as f ( x,y ) = ln ( x + ϵ y + ϵ ) , also meets these assumption.

## EC.7.1. Price Fairness with Hard Constraint

To deal with the general discrepancy function, we only need to adapt the subroutine ExploreUnconstrainedOPT (Algorithm 2) to the following Algorithm 6. The key difference between Algorithm 6 and Algorithm 2, is that in Line 4 of Algorithm 6, we search the beginning point instead of the middle point.

We then present the theoretical results as follows. Since the proof of the Lemma EC.15 and Lemma EC.16 is similar to that in Section EC.1.2, we omit the proof details.

In analogous to Lemma EC.4, the following lemma establishes the relation between the price difference of the constrained optimal solution and that of the unconstrained optimal solution.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In analogous to Lemma EC.5,the following lemma provide bounds for the ξ parameter which is used in the algorithm to control the price gaps between the two customer groups.

## Algorithm 6: ExploreConstrainedOPTGeneralDiscrepancy

- Input : the estimated unconstrained optimal prices ̂ p ♯ 1 and ̂ p ♯ 2 , assuming that ̂ p ♯ 1 ≤ ̂ p ♯ 2 (without loss of generality)
- Output: the estimated constrained optimal prices ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 1 ξ ← max {| f ( ̂ p ♯ 1 , ̂ p ♯ 2 ) | -8 L f T -1 / 5 , 0 } ; 2 J ←⌈ ( p -p ) T 1 5 ⌉ and create J price checkpoints ℓ 1 , ℓ 2 , . . . , ℓ J where ℓ j ← p + j J ( p -p ); 3 for each ℓ j do 4 Repeat the following offerings for 6 T 2 / 5 ln T selling periods: offer price p 1 ( j ) ← ℓ j to customer group 1 and price p 2 ( j ) ← min { p, f -1 ( ℓ j ; λξ ) } to customer group 2; 5 Denote the average demand from customer group i ∈{ 1 , 2 } by ̂ d i ( j ); 6 ̂ R ( j ) ← ̂ d 1 ( j )( p 1 ( j ) -c ) + ̂ d 2 ( j )( p 2 ( j ) -c ); 7 j ∗ ← argmax j ∈{ 1 , 2 ,...,J } { ̂ R ( j ) } ; 8 return ̂ p ∗ 1 ← p 1 ( j ∗ ) and ̂ p ∗ 2 ← p 2 ( j ∗ );

Lemma EC.16. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ 1 -p ♯ 1 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 and | ̂ p ♯ 2 -p ♯ 2 | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 , we have that λξ ≤ λ | f ( p ♯ 1 , p ♯ 2 ) | and λξ ≥ max { 0 , λ | f ( p ♯ 1 , p ♯ 2 ) | -16 L f T -1 / 5 } .

In analogous to Lemma EC.6, the following lemma shows that our discretization scheme always guarantees that there is a price check point to approximate the constrained optimal prices.

Lemma EC.17. There exists ˜ j ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } such that both p 1 ( ˜ j ) , p 2 ( ˜ j ) ∈ [ p, p ] and | p 1 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 1 | ≤ T -1 / 5 , | p 2 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 2 | ≤ 17 L 2 f T -1 / 5 .

Proof. By the definition of J , we know there exists ˜ j ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } such that both p 1 ( ˜ j ) , p 2 ( ˜ j ) ∈ [ p, p ] and | p 1 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 1 | ≤ T -1 / 5 . Thus it is sufficient to show such ˜ j meets the requirement for p 2 .

By Lipschitz property EC.1(b), Lemma EC.16 and the choice of | p 1 ( ˜ j ) -p ∗ 1 | ≤ T -1 / 5 , we have:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, we prove this lemma. □

Note this lemma is the same as Lemma EC.6 up to constant factors of L 2 f . Thus with such lemma we immediately get the following theorem. We omit the proof since it is almost the same as the proof of Theorem 3.

Theorem EC.2. With probability (1 -O ( T -1 )) , modified Algorithm 1 (substituting Algorithm 3 by Algorithm 6) satisfies the fairness constraint and its regret is at most O ( T 4 5 log 2 T ) . Here, the O ( · ) notation only hides the polynomial dependence on p , K , L f and 1 /C .

## EC.7.2. General Fairness Measure with Soft Constraint

We may combine the techniques in Section EC.7.1 and Section 7 to deal with the soft fairness constraint with both a general discrepancy function f and a general fairness measure M i ( · ). In this case, we may obtain a similar T 4 / 5 -type soft regret bound. We omit the details for this setting since the techniques used here are quite repetitive and their combination is quite straightforward.

## EC.8. Extension: the Mult-group Setting

In this section, we extend our algorithms and theoretical results to encompass multi-group setting in terms of both price fairness and general fairness cases. Specifically, we adopt the fairness constraints in Cohen et al. (2022): | M i ( p i ) -M j ( p j ) | ≤ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N ∣ ∣ ∣ M i ′ ( p ♯ i ′ ) -M j ′ ( p ♯ j ′ ) ∣ ∣ ∣ for all 1 ≤ i &lt; j ≤ N pairs.

We first define the unconstrained optimal solution in the multi-group setting as follows.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Following the definition in Cohen et al. (2022), let { p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 , . . . , p ∗ N } denote the fairness-aware clairvoyant solution, i.e., the optimal solution to the following static optimization problem,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The seller would like to minimize the regret , which is the difference between the expected total revenue and the fairness-aware clairvoyant solution:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where p ( t ) i , i ∈ [ N ] satisfy the hard constraint defined in Eq. (EC.40).

For general fairness measure, the seller aims to minimize the following cumulative penalized regret:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first term is the standard regret, the second term is the penalty term for violating the fairness constraint, and γ is a pre-defined parameter to balance between the regret and the fairness constraint and assumed to be O (1).

Throughout this section, we will make the following standard assumptions on demand functions and fairness measures, which simply extend Assumption 1 from two groups to N groups:

Assumption EC.2. (a) The demand-price functions are monotonically decreasing and injective

Lipschitz, i.e., there exists a constant K ≥ 1 such that for each group i ∈ [ N ] , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- (b) The revenue-demand functions are strongly concave, i.e., there exists a constant C &gt; 0 such that for each group i ∈ [ N ] , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- (c) The fairness measures are Lipschitz, i.e., there exists a constant K ′ such that for each group i ∈ [ N ] , it holds that:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- (d) There exists a constant M ≥ 1 such that the noisy observation M ( t ) i ∈ [0 , M ] for every selling period t and customer group i ∈ [ N ] .

## EC.8.1. Price Fairness for Multiple Groups

Our fairness-aware pricing for multi-group algorithm (see Algorithm 7) has the same structure as Algorithm 1. The ExploreUnconstrainedOPTMultiGroup subroutine (Algorithm 8) is adapted from ExploreUnconstrainedOPT (Algorithm 2) by simply considering N groups, while the ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup subroutine (Algorithm 9) have several significant differences with ExploreConstrainedOPT (Algorithm 3).

In general, after setting up the checking points as possible mean prices, ExploreConstrainedOPT (Algorithm 3) sets up a fairness-aware price range(i.e., [ ℓ j -λξ 2 , ℓ j + λξ 2 ]) and offers the boundary prices to two groups, while ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup (Algorithm 9) bounds all customer groups within such fairness-aware price range. For the group(s) with an unconstrained optimal price learned within the range, the subroutine offers the learned price. Otherwise, the closest possible price in the range is offered to the group(s) whose learned price is located outside the range. Thus, ExploreConstrainedOPT (Algorithm 3) can be seen as a special case of ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup (Algorithm 9) with a group size, N , equals to 2.

For Algorithm 8, we prove the following upper bounds on the number of selling periods used by the algorithm and its estimation error.

## Algorithm 7: Fairness-aware Dynamic Pricing for MultiGroup

- 1 For each group z ∈ [ N ], run ExploreUnconstrainedOPTMultiGroup (Algorithm 3) separately with the input of group z , and obtain the estimation of the optimal price without fairness constraint ̂ p ♯ i .
- 2 Given ( ̂ p ♯ 1 , ̂ p ♯ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ♯ N ), run ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup (Algorithm 9), and obtain ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ).
- 3 For each of the remaining selling periods, offer ̂ p ∗ z to the customer group z .

## Algorithm 8: ExploreUnconstrainedOPTMultiGroup

Input : the customer group index z ∈ [ N ]

Output: the estimated unconstrained optimal price ̂ p ♯ for group z

- 1 p L ← p , p R ← p , r ← 0;
- 2 while | p L -p R | &gt; 4 T -1 / 5 do
- 3 r ← r +1;
- 4 p m 1 ← 2 3 p L + 1 3 p R , p m 2 ← 1 3 p L + 2 3 p R ;
- 5 Offer price p m 1 to all customer groups for 25 K 4 p 2 C 2 T 4 / 5 ln( NT ) selling periods and denote the average demand from customer group z by ̂ d m 1 ;
- 6 Offer price p m 2 to all customer groups for 25 K 4 p 2 C 2 T 4 / 5 ln( NT ) selling periods and denote the average demand from customer group z by ̂ d m 2 ;
- 7 if ̂ d m 1 ( p m 1 -c ) &gt; ̂ d m 2 ( p m 2 -c ) then p R ← p m 2 ; else p L ← p m 1 ;
- 8 return ̂ p ♯ = 1 2 ( p L + p R );

Theorem EC.3. For any input z ∈ [ N ] , Algorithm 8 uses at most O ( K 4 p 2 C 2 NT 4 5 log( NT ) log( pT )) selling periods and satisfies the fairness constraint during each period. Let ̂ p ♯ z be the output of the procedure. With probability (1 -O ( T -2 log( pT ))) , it holds that | ̂ p ♯ z -p ♯ z | ≤ 4 T -1 5 for all z ∈ [ N ] . Here, only universal constants are hidden in the O ( · ) notations.

We omit the proof of Theorem EC.3 since it can be directly adapted from Theorem 1. We then state the following guarantee for Algorithm 9.

Theorem EC.4. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ z -p ♯ z | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 for all z ∈ [ N ] . Algorithm 9 uses at most O ( pNT 3 / 5 log( NT )) selling periods and satisfies the price fairness constraint during each selling period. With probability (1 -O ( pT -2 )) , the procedure returns a pair of price ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ) such

## Algorithm 9: ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup

Input : the estimated unconstrained optimal prices ( ̂ p ♯ 1 , ̂ p ♯ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ♯ N ). Output: the estimated constrained optimal prices ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ). 1 ξ ← max { max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N {| ̂ p ♯ i ′ -̂ p ♯ j ′ |} -8 T -1 / 5 , 0 } ; 2 J ←⌈ ( p -p ) T 1 5 ⌉ and create J price checkpoints ℓ 1 , ℓ 2 , . . . , ℓ J where ℓ j ← p + j J ( p -p ); 3 for each ℓ j do 4 Repeat the following offerings for 6 T 2 / 5 ln( NT ) selling periods: 5 for z =1 , 2 , . . . , N do 6 if ̂ p ♯ z ∈ ( ℓ j -λξ 2 , ℓ j + λξ 2 ) then 7 offer price p z ( j ) ← ̂ p ♯ z to customer group z ; 8 else if ̂ p ♯ z ≤ ℓ j -λξ 2 then 9 offer price p z ( j ) ← max { p, ℓ j -λξ 2 } to customer group z ; 10 else 11 offer price p z ( j ) ← min { p, ℓ j + λξ 2 } to customer group z ; 12 Denote the average demand from customer group z ∈ [ N ] by ̂ d z ( j ); 13 ̂ R ( j ) ← ∑ N z =1 ( ̂ d i ( j )( p z ( j ) -c )); 14 j ∗ ← argmax j ∈{ 1 , 2 ,...,J } { ̂ R ( j ) } ; 15 return ̂ p ∗ z ← p z ( j ∗ ) for all z ∈ [ N ];

that for any i, j ∈ [ N ] , | ̂ p ∗ i -̂ p ∗ j | ≤ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here, only universal constants are hidden in the O ( · ) notations.

Based on Theorems EC.3 and EC.4, we are ready to state the regret bound of our main algorithm.

Theorem EC.5. With probability (1 -O ( T -1 )) , Algorithm 7 satisfies the fairness constraint and its regret is at most O ( NT 4 5 log( NT ) log T ) . Here, the O ( · ) notation only hides the polynomial dependence on p , K , and 1 /C .

Since the proof is similar to that of Theorem 3, we omit the proof of Theorem EC.5.

## EC.8.2. Proof of Theorem EC.4 for ExploreConstrainedOPTMultiGroup

First, the following lemma upper bounds the number of time periods used by the algorithm.

Lemma EC.18. Algorithm 9 uses at most O ( pNT 3 / 5 log( NT )) selling periods, where only a universal constant is hidden in the O ( · ) notation.

We next turn to prove the (near-)optimality of the estimated prices ̂ p ∗ z for all z ∈ [ N ]. To this end, we first establish the following key relation between between the constrained optimal solution and the unconstrained optimal solution.

Lemma EC.19. There exist two constant L ∗ &lt;R ∗ ∈ [min z p ♯ z , max z p ♯ z ] such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof. In this proof we assume p ♯ 1 ≤ p ♯ 2 ≤··· ≤ p ♯ N without loss of generality as the other case can be similarly handled by symmetry. We decompose the proof of this lemma into three steps as follows.

Step I: prove p ∗ z ∈ [ p ♯ 1 , p ♯ N ] . Since R z ( d ) is a unimodal function and d z ( p ) is a monotonically decreasing function, we have that R z ( p ) = R z ( d z ( p )) is a unimodal function for all z ∈ [ N ].

By the definition of optimal solution ( p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 , . . . , p ∗ N ), we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

If p ∗ z ∈ [ p ♯ 1 , p ♯ N ] , ∀ z ∈ [ N ], we obtain the desired conclusion. And if one of the following three cases occur, we will get a contradiction.

- Case 1: there exist z and z ′ such that p ∗ z &lt;p ♯ 1 and p ∗ z ′ &gt;p ♯ N . In this case, p ∗ z ′ -p ∗ z &gt;p ♯ N -p ♯ 1 ≥ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | , which contradicts with the constraint of optimal solution.
- Case 2: p ∗ z &gt;p ♯ 1 , ∀ z ∈ [ N ] and there exists z ∈ [ N ] such that p ∗ z &gt;p ♯ N . In this case, we could define a new solution, which have large value that than the optimal solution, to get the contradiction. Specifically, we can use p ♯ N to substitute p ∗ z for all z ∈ [ N ] such that p ∗ z &gt;p ♯ N . And by the unimodal property of R z ( p ), we could have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- Case 3: p ∗ z &lt;p ♯ N , ∀ z ∈ [ N ] and there exists z ∈ [ N ] such that p ∗ z &lt;p ♯ 1 . In a similar way with Case 2, we can also get a contradiction in this case.

Step II: prove max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | = λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | . Since we already have max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | ≤ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | , we only need to prove there exist 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N such that | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | = λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | .

With the fact that p ∗ z ∈ [ p ♯ 1 , p ♯ N ] , ∀ z ∈ [ N ], we claim that | p ∗ N -p ∗ 1 | =max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | , since if it is not true we can move p ∗ 1 and p ∗ N to the boundary of ( p ∗ 1 , p ∗ 2 , . . . , p ∗ N ) to get a larger revenue.

By the same argument as Eq. (EC.5) and Eq. (EC.6) in the proof of Lemma EC.4, we can obtain that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, we get a conclusion that p ∗ N -p ∗ 1 =max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | = λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | = λ ( p ♯ N -p ♯ 1 ) .

Step III: discuss the value of p ∗ z . Let L ∗ = p ∗ 1 and R ∗ = p ∗ N . Then we separate z ∈ [ N ] into the following three case according to value of p ♯ z , and discuss the value of p ∗ z individually.

- Case 1: p ♯ z ∈ ( L ∗ , R ∗ ) . In this case, since the unconstrained optimal solution p ♯ z of R z ( p ) is contained in the interval ( L ∗ , R ∗ ), we claim that p ∗ z = p ♯ z . If it is not true, we could use p ♯ z to substitute p ∗ z and get a larger revenue without violating the constraint max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ∗ i ′ -p ∗ j ′ | ≤ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N | p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | .
- Case 2: p ♯ z ≤ L ∗ . Since by the result in Step II, we have p ∗ z ∈ [ L ∗ , R ∗ ] , ∀ z ∈ [ N ]. In this case, if p ∗ z &gt;L ∗ and by the unimodal property of R z ( p ), we could have R z ( L ∗ ) &gt;R z ( p ∗ z ), where we get a contradiction. Therefore, we have p ∗ z = L ∗ for all z ∈ [ N ] satisfying p ♯ z ≤ L ∗ .
- Case 3: p ♯ z ≥ R ∗ . In a similar way with Case 2, we have p ∗ z = R ∗ for all z ∈ [ N ] satisfying p ♯ z ≥ R ∗ .

Therefore, by the conclusion of Step III, this lemma is proved. □

The following lemma provides bounds for the ξ parameter which is used in Algorithm 9 to control the price gaps among customer groups.

Lemma EC.20. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ z -p ♯ z | ≤ 4 T -1 / 5 for all z ∈ [ N ] , we have that λξ ≤ λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N {| p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ |} and λξ ≥ max { λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N {| p ♯ i ′ -p ♯ j ′ | , 0 } -16 T -1 / 5 } .

With these technical lemmas in hand, we can easily prove Theorem EC.4.

## EC.8.3. General Fairness Measure under the Soft Constraints

In this section, we study fairness-aware dynamic pricing in the multi-group setting with general fairness measure { M i ( p ) } and soft constraints.

We first adapt Algorithm 4 and Algorithm 5 to the multi-group version, Algorithm 10 and Algorithm 11. Then, we state the following guarantee for Algorithm 11.

## Algorithm 10: Fairness-aware Dynamic Pricing for General Fairness Measure in Multigroup Case

- 1 For each group i ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , N } , run ExploreUnconstrainedOPTMultiGroup separately with the input z = i , and obtain the estimation of the optimal price without fairness constraint ̂ p ♯ i .
- 2 Given ( ̂ p ♯ 1 , ̂ p ♯ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ♯ N ), run ExploreConstrainedOPTGeneralMultiGroup (Algorithm 11), and obtain ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ).
- 3 For each of the remaining selling periods, offer ̂ p ∗ i to the customer group i .

## Algorithm 11: ExploreConstrainedOPTGeneralMultiGroup

Input : the estimated unconstrained optimal prices ( ̂ p ♯ 1 , ̂ p ♯ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ♯ N );

Output: the estimated constrained optimal prices ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N );

- 1 J ←⌈ ( p -p ) T 1 5 ⌉ and create J price checkpoints ℓ 1 , ℓ 2 , . . . , ℓ J where ℓ j ← p + j J ( p -p );
- 2 for each ℓ j do
- 3 Repeat the following offering for 6 T 2 / 5 ln( NT ) selling periods: offer price ℓ j to N customer groups;
- 4 For each customer group i ∈ [ N ], denote the average demand from the customer group ̂ d i ( ℓ j ), and the average of the observed fairness measurement value by ̂ M i ( ℓ j );
- 5 Let ̂ R i ( ℓ j ) ← ̂ d i ( ℓ j ) · ( ℓ j -c ), for each i ∈ [ N ];
- 6 For each i ∈ [ N ], round up ̂ p ♯ i to the nearest price checkpoint, namely ℓ t i ;
- 7 For all tuples j 1 , j 2 , . . . , j N ∈{ 1 , 2 , . . . , J } , let ̂ G ( ℓ j 1 , ℓ j 2 , . . . , ℓ j N ) ← ∑ N i =1 ̂ R i ( ℓ j i ) -γ ∑ 1 ≤ m&lt;n ≤ N max (∣ ∣ ∣ ̂ M m ( ℓ j m ) -̂ M n ( ℓ j n ) ∣ ∣ ∣ -λ max 1 ≤ i ′ &lt;j ′ ≤ N ∣ ∣ ∣ ̂ M i ′ ( ℓ t i ′ ) -̂ M j ′ ( ℓ t j ′ ) ∣ ∣ ∣ , 0 ) ;
- 8 Let ( j ∗ 1 , j ∗ 2 , . . . , j ∗ N ) ← argmax j 1 ,j 2 ,...,j N ∈{ 1 , 2 ,...,J } { ̂ G ( ℓ j 1 , ℓ j 2 , . . . , ℓ j N ) } ;
- 9 return ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ) ← ( ℓ j ∗ 1 , ℓ j ∗ 2 , . . . , ℓ j ∗ N );

Theorem EC.6. Suppose that | ̂ p ♯ i -p ♯ i | &lt; 4 T -1 5 for any i ∈ [ N ] . Also assume that γ ≤ O (1) . Algorithm 11 uses at most O ( NT 3 5 log( NT )) selling periods in total, and with probability at least (1 -O ( T -1 )) , the procedure returns a pair of prices ( ̂ p ∗ 1 , ̂ p ∗ 2 , . . . , ̂ p ∗ N ) such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here the O ( · ) notation hides the polynomial dependence on p , γ , K , K ′ , M and C .

Combining Theorem EC.3 and Theorem EC.6, we are ready to state the regret bound of the algorithm.

Theorem EC.7. Assume that γ ≤ O (1) . With probability (1 -O ( T -1 )) , the cumulative penalized regret of Algorithm 10 is at most Reg soft T ≤ O ( N 2 T 4 / 5 log T log( NT )) . Here the O ( · ) notation hides the polynomial dependence on p , γ , K , K ′ , M , and C .

Since the proof is similar to that of Theorem 6, we omit the proof of Theorem EC.7.

## EC.8.4. Proof of Theorem EC.6 for ExploreConstrainedOPTGeneralMultiGroup

First, the following lemma, whose proof is the same as that of Lemma EC.9, upper bounds the number of the selling periods used by the algorithm.

Lemma EC.23. Algorithm 11 uses at most O ( pNT 3 5 log( NT )) selling periods, where only a universal constant is hidden in O ( · ) notation.

We then turn to upper bound the penalized regret incurred by the estimated prices ̂ p ∗ 1 and ̂ p ∗ 2 . Define

## EC.9. Additional Numerical Results

FDP-DL

Tri-Section

In this section, as the supplement of the numerical experiments in Section 8 of the main paper, we present the numerical results of Algorithm 1 under the linear demand function (Figure EC.1) and the inverse proportional demand function (Figure EC.2). The detailed definitions of the above two types of demand functions can be found at the beginning of Section 8. y =0.85087x-0.32902

We use the same log-scaled axes and linear-fitting results as in Section 8 to better illustrate the relationship between the regret and the total number of time periods. 10 9.5 y = 0.78682x-0.40705 9.5 ₴

We see that when λ =0, the two baseline algorithms theoretically achieve √ T -type regret and indeed beat our algorithm(FDP-DL) in the experiments. On the other hand, our algorithm maintains a stable T 4 / 5 -type regret under both types of demand functions and for various λ . Our algorithm performs significantly better than the baselines when λ becomes greater than 0. Tri-Section In(T) Tri-Section In(T)

Regarding the results of the inverse proportional demand function, we note that since such functions do not meet Assumptions 1(a) and 1(b), the corresponding numerical results illustrate the robustness of our algorithm even when some of the theoretical assumptions are not fully satisfied.
